ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general sponge cake class; no specific steamed variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN covers sponge cake as a general category; steamed preparation variant not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for steamed sponge cake; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of steamed sponge cake is one slice or one piece weighing approximately 60 g, as commonly sold at Singapore hawker stalls and bakeries.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed Sponge Cake — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Steamed Sponge Cake (cr_id F02010016). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this primary regional source.

gi_source.title: Sponge cake, plain — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 67 (medium) is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain sponge cake, which is the closest defensible analogous food. Steamed preparation is typical for this Singaporean variant; the egg-and-flour base is compositionally similar to a plain sponge cake. Glycemic load per 60 g serving calculated as: (67 × 42.8 × 0.60) / 100 = 17.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (24.5 g/100 g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.5 g/100 g) promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and weight gain, independently elevating blood pressure risk; sodium is also moderate at 266 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit steamed sponge cake due to its high sugar load (24.5 g/100 g) and moderate sodium (266 mg/100 g), both of which contribute to elevated blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 24.5 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrate is 42.8 g/100 g with only 0.5 g dietary fibre, producing a medium GI of 67 and a glycaemic load of 17.2 per 60 g serving, causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid steamed sponge cake or limit portions to very small amounts, as its high sugar content, low fibre, and medium-to-high glycaemic load will cause significant postprandial blood glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High refined sugar (24.5 g/100 g) and moderate sodium (266 mg/100 g) contribute to dyslipidaemia, weight gain, and elevated blood pressure, all of which are independent cardiovascular risk factors; saturated fat is estimated at approximately 3.2 g/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit steamed sponge cake consumption due to its high sugar content and moderate sodium, which together promote weight gain, dyslipidaemia, and blood pressure elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar at 24.5 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g RA pro-inflammatory threshold; no anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, fibre >3 g) are present to offset the inflammatory burden.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid steamed sponge cake as its high refined sugar content promotes systemic inflammatory mediators and no anti-inflammatory nutrients are present to counterbalance this effect.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed sponge cake (鸡蛋糕, Ji Dan Gao) is a traditional Chinese-Singaporean dessert and snack made by steaming a batter of eggs, wheat flour, sugar, and oil until it achieves a soft, moist, and springy texture. Unlike Western oven-baked sponge cakes, the steaming method produces a pale, tender crumb with a slightly denser bite and a subtly eggy, sweet flavour. It is widely sold at Singapore hawker centres, traditional bakeries, and wet markets, and is commonly consumed as a breakfast item or afternoon snack. Per 100 g, it provides 319 kcal, 7.1 g protein, 13.3 g fat, 42.8 g carbohydrate, and 24.5 g sugar, with 266 mg sodium and only 0.5 g dietary fibre. The high sugar content and low fibre make it nutritionally unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis. Its glycaemic index is approximately 67 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of about 17.2 per standard 60 g serving. Calcium is present at 65 mg/100 g, contributed by eggs and flour. The food is vegetarian, halal-friendly, and dairy-free, but contains eggs and gluten allergens.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat steamed sponge cake?
Is steamed sponge cake suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of sponge cake?
Is steamed sponge cake gluten free?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02010016), which is the primary approved regional source for Singaporean foods. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (plain sponge cake) in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source. Confidence is high for macros and secondary nutrients; GI is an analogous estimate with documented basis.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 3.2 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for plain sponge cake (NDB No. 18135) as a secondary reference, since HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food and are not available from a directly matching approved source. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g × serving_g) / 10000 = (67 × 42.8 × 60) / 10000 = 17.2. The input record listed GI source as 'Embedded GI Database' with confidence 0.316; this has been replaced with the University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for plain sponge cake to comply with approved source policy. The hypertension caution in the input incorrectly referred to this solid food as a 'sugar-sweetened beverage'; this has been corrected in the output.
